vulnerabilities.title: increaseReserve Bypasses Accumulator Accounting, Causing _accountedBalance() Underflow That Permanently Bricks contributePrizeTokens
vulnerabilities.summary: The increaseReserve() function (line 498) allows anyone to deposit prize tokens directly into the reserve by calling _reserve += _amount and safeTransferFrom. However, it does NOT update the totalAccumulator. The _accountedBalance() function (line 743) computes (obs.available + obs.disbursed) - _totalWithdrawn, where obs.available + obs.disbursed only reflects tokens added via contributePrizeTokens (which updates the accumulator), while _totalWithdrawn reflects ALL outflows via _transfer() inclu
vulnerabilities.impact: Permanent denial of service on contributePrizeTokens. All vaults relying on this PrizePool can no longer contribute yield as prizes. Existing unclaimed prizes may still be claimed, but no new liquidity enters, causing a gradual drain of all remaining tier liquidity with no replenishment. This is a permanent loss-of-funds scenario for all depositors across all vaults that depend on this PrizePool instance.
vulnerabilities.proof_of_concept: 1. The PrizePool operates normally with contributions flowing through contributePrizeTokens. Accumulator tracks 10,000 tokens total (obs.available + obs.disbursed = 10000). 5,000 tokens have been claimed (_totalWithdrawn = 5000). _accountedBalance() = 5000. 2. A well-meaning donor (or the protocol itself) calls increaseReserve(6000), depositing 6000 tokens into the reserve. The accumulator is NOT updated. _reserve increases by 6000. 3. Over subsequent draws, prize claims on underfunded tiers trigger _consumeLiquidity to dip into the inflated reserve. Each such claim calls _transfer which increments _totalWithdrawn. 4. After enough reserve-funded prizes are claimed (total reserve consumed >= 1), _totalWithdrawn exceeds 10000 (the accumulator total). 5. _accountedBalance() attempts 10000 - (>10000) = underflow revert. 6. contributePrizeTokens permanently reverts, preventing all future prize funding. The pool becomes a zombie. No privileged role is needed: increaseReserve has no access control, and prize claiming is permissionless via Vault.claimPrizes.

vulnerabilities.title: setDrawManager Front-Running Allows Permanent Seizure of Draw Manager Role, Enabling Theft of Reserve and Prize Manipulation
vulnerabilities.summary: When the PrizePool constructor is called with params.drawManager = address(0) (a supported deployment path per line 278-281), the setDrawManager() function (line 299-306) becomes callable by anyone exactly once. The function has NO access control -- no ownership check, no deployer check, no timelock. Once set, the draw manager cannot be changed (DrawManagerAlreadySet revert). The draw manager controls: (1) closeDraw -- choosing the winningRandomNumber that determines all prize winners, (2) withd
vulnerabilities.impact: Complete theft of all reserve funds. Manipulation of all prize outcomes allowing theft of tier liquidity through controlled prize wins. If any contributions were made before the legitimate setDrawManager call, those funds are immediately at risk. The PrizePool must be redeployed and abandoned, potentially losing all previously deposited prize tokens.
vulnerabilities.proof_of_concept: 1. Deployer creates PrizePool with drawManager = address(0), planning to set it in a follow-up transaction (or via a deployment script that is not atomic). 2. Attacker monitors the mempool, sees the deployment, and front-runs with setDrawManager(attackerAddress). 3. The attacker is now the permanent, irrevocable draw manager. 4. Attacker calls withdrawReserve to steal all reserve funds. 5. Attacker calls closeDraw with a crafted winningRandomNumber that makes their controlled addresses win high-tier prizes, then claims them. 6. Since drawManager is immutable once set, the pool must be abandoned, losing any pre-contributed funds.

vulnerabilities.title: setLiquidationPair Grants Unlimited Approval of Underlying Asset to External LiquidationPair Contract
vulnerabilities.summary: The setLiquidationPair() function (line 665) grants type(uint256).max approval of the vault's underlying asset (e.g., USDC) to the newly set LiquidationPair contract (line 677: _asset.safeApprove(address(liquidationPair_), type(uint256).max)). This creates a persistent unlimited approval of the vault's underlying asset to an external, replaceable contract. If the LiquidationPair contract has any vulnerability that allows arbitrary token transfers, or if a future LP replacement is set to a malici
vulnerabilities.impact: Theft of all idle underlying assets in the vault. The attack surface is expanded because any vulnerability in any LiquidationPair contract becomes a vulnerability in the vault's asset custody. Idle assets exist during normal operation: after deposits before yield vault sweep, during liquidation flows, and from any direct transfers. The approval persists and is refreshed each time setLiquidationPair is called.
vulnerabilities.proof_of_concept: 1. Vault owner calls setLiquidationPair with a standard-looking LP contract that has a subtle vulnerability (e.g., an arbitrary call in a callback, or a missing access control on an emergency function). 2. An attacker exploits the vulnerability in the LP contract to call IERC20(underlying).transferFrom(vault, attacker, vaultBalance). 3. The vault's type(uint256).max approval allows this transfer to succeed. 4. All idle underlying assets in the vault (assets from recent deposits not yet swept to yield vault, assets present during the liquidation flow per line 578-582) are drained. 5. Depositors lose their assets that were in transit.
